N started crying almost immediately. Life is so unfair, Filiz. This is so unfair.

F said, “No, I don’t think it’s anything. I really don’t.” But his voice, his whole energy on the phone, changed completely. I knew him well enough to know he looked it up the moment we hung up.

M called me a little later, after he had done his own research. He is deeply religious — faith is the center of his life. But that day he was angry. I’m angry at God, Filiz. He hasn’t given you things other people take for granted — and now this on top of everything? He shouldn’t do this. His voice broke a little.

I have had a great life — hard, yes, but genuinely, fully great. If today were my last day, I would have no remorse. My close friends know this about me! I don’t just exist. I live.

I understood why they were reacting this way. They know my life. They know that I have navigated most of it without the safety net that most people don’t even notice they have — the people who are simply there, who you don’t have to explain anything to, who would show up.

My friends are that for me now. But they also know what it cost to get here. And they were frightened for someone who has already carried more than her share.

I listened to both of them. I told them it would be okay. I didn’t know yet if that was true, but it felt like the right thing to say.

Only later did I notice that I had spent those conversations on the other side of the equation — the one doing the reassuring, not the one being reassured.

I didn’t mind. That is just how I am built.

But I also knew, somewhere, that I needed to be as careful with myself as my friends were being with me.

I am sensitive but I am also strong. I accepted a long time ago that life is not fair. I have always tried to live fully in whatever moment I am in. That is how my grandmother raised me.

I was not going to sit at home for two weeks feeling sorry for myself.

The week after the appointment, we bought tickets to New York. We went. We walked, ate, visited every bookstore I loved, spent hours in museums in front of paintings I love. We stayed in Hoboken and every morning I woke up to the Manhattan skyline.

The MS thought came back occasionally. If you have MS, you can collapse suddenly. That was frightening. But if I had MS, there was nothing I could do about it. I would only know when I knew.

Then came the MRI.

There was a problem.

The machine was not the kind where only your head goes in. My whole body had to be inside.

I have claustrophobia.

I undressed completely. They gave me a white garment — ugly, unironed, freshly washed. I lay down on the table. The machine began to move slowly, pulling me inside.

A few minutes in, I said I needed to get out. I was breathing too fast. The technician said: I don’t think we can do this, you’re not looking well.

I really couldn’t. My heart was trying to leave my chest. It’s a terrible machine — it makes terrible sounds and you cannot move at all.

But I genuinely needed to know. Whether I had MS or not. The uncertainty was one thing — I can tolerate uncertainty. But this uncertainty was different.

I said: I’ll do it.

I lay back down. The technician said: I don’t think so, but okay, let’s try.

I closed my eyes. I did not open them for one hour.

I could feel tears wanting to come but I held them. I was going to learn the truth, whatever it was.

My heart was beating loud enough to feel in my chest for the first four or five minutes.

And then, from somewhere, a memory came — something I hadn’t thought about in twenty-four years.

My grandmother had surgery once, when I was a child. I stayed with my aunt during that time. When we went to visit her afterward, I asked: Grandma, what did they do to you?

She told me they put her in a closed machine for a long time, then operated, removed the bad things. She had serious heart problems and had gone through open heart surgery.

I asked: Weren’t you scared? In the closed machine?

She said: I was scared. But then I thought of you, and it passed. ( she never denied the emotions who makes us huma, she was not like other parent she ver said things like, i was not afraid etc. she always accepted every feeling, and i think that is why my emotional regulation is so healthy)

That memory came to me in the machine.

I thought: She was scared too. She thought of me and it passed. I’m scared. I’ll think of her.

It will pass. A few more minutes. You’re almost there, Filiz.

When the sixty minutes ended, the white garment was soaked through.

The technician said: I thought you couldn’t do it. You’re pretty tough.

It was not MS.

It was something called pseudotumor cerebri. Hypothyroidism and extra weight were putting pressure on my spine. Not even a real tumor. There was treatment. There was a solution.

Everyone was very happy. I was happy too.

But I had learned something.

I needed to take better care of myself. Everything outside of me — every obligation, every responsibility, every job, every person — was a fraction of my life.

The main thing was me.

Me » everything else.

I had always been sensitive to other people, always tried to help, always taken on more than I should. But this moment was clear: I needed to take care of myself. Physically, psychologically, emotionally.

I kept that promise to myself for a long time.

Then — being in a foreign country, the workload, the school, the pressure — somewhere the thread broke again. Not as badly as before, but I still wasn’t meeting all my own needs.

A few weeks ago, my body reminded me again — iron deficiency, heart palpitations, shortness of breath.

I remembered the girl in the MRI machine who held herself together for sixty minutes so she could learn the truth.

Don’t forget her, I told myself. Your degree, your work, other people — all of it is a fraction of your life. You come first.

I don’t want my body to have to remind me again.

Everything else can wait. Everyone else can wait.

I am only going to spend my energy on things that make me happy, on things that move me toward a better version of myself.

I already don’t keep people around me who exhaust me. But the ones I’ve felt obligated to — the ones who drain me for no real reason — I’m going to give them only what’s necessary. Nothing more.

I love myself. I want to spend my life with the people and things that make me feel that.

No more chasing. No more fixing. No more running after things that aren’t already moving toward me.

My happiness is the most important debt I owe.

And I intend to pay it.
entry.summary: I owe myself happiness. This is how I learned that.

entry.title: The Renormalization Group — The Deepest Idea in Physics

link.title: The Renormalization Group — The Deepest Idea in Physics

entry.content:
There is an idea in physics that is so deep that once you understand it, you cannot unsee it.

It is everywhere.

In the flow of a turbulent river. In the behavior of magnets near the critical point. In the reason quantum field theory works at all. In the structure of the Standard Model. In the hierarchy of scales in nature.

The idea is the renormalization group.

It is not a group in the mathematical sense — it does not have inverses. It is not a renormalization in the original sense — it is not about removing infinities.

It is a way of thinking about how physics changes with scale — how the description of a system transforms as you zoom in or zoom out — and what remains invariant under this transformation.

It is, I will argue, the deepest idea in physics.

I. The Problem of Scale

Physics has a problem.

At every scale of nature, there is structure.

At \(10^{-35}\) m: quantum gravity, Planck scale. At \(10^{-18}\) m: quarks, gluons, electroweak unification. At \(10^{-15}\) m: protons, neutrons, nuclear physics. At \(10^{-10}\) m: atoms, atomic physics. At \(10^{-9}\) m: molecules, chemistry. At \(10^{-6}\) m: cells, biology. At \(10^{0}\) m: human scale, classical mechanics. At \(10^{20}\) m: galaxies, cosmology.

Fifty orders of magnitude of structure.

The miracle: you do not need to know all scales to describe physics at any given scale.

To describe the motion of a billiard ball, you do not need to know the positions of all its atoms.

To describe atomic physics, you do not need to know the internal structure of the nucleus.

To describe nuclear physics, you do not need to know the details of quark confinement.

Physics at each scale is largely autonomous — decoupled from the details of smaller scales.

Why?

The renormalization group explains why.

II. Wilson’s Revolutionary Idea

Kenneth Wilson (Nobel Prize 1982) transformed our understanding of quantum field theory and statistical mechanics in the early 1970s.

His key insight:

The physics at long wavelengths (low energies) is determined by an effective theory — obtained by integrating out the short-wavelength (high-energy) degrees of freedom.

This is Wilsonian renormalization.

Consider a system of spins on a lattice — a ferromagnet. Lattice spacing: \(a\). Total size: \(L\).

The degrees of freedom: spins \(s_i\) at each lattice site.

Wilson’s procedure — block spin renormalization:

  1. Coarse-grain: average spins in blocks of size \(ba\) (\(b > 1\), typically \(b = 2\)).

  2. Rescale: redefine the length unit: \(x o x/b\) so the new lattice has the same spacing \(a\).

  3. Renormalize fields: rescale the block spin to restore canonical normalization.

The result: a new effective theory with the same form as the original, but with renormalized coupling constants.

The couplings flow: \(g o g'(g, b)\)

This is the renormalization group transformation: \(\mathcal{R}_b: \{g\} o \{g'\}\)

It forms a semigroup — composition works, but there are no inverses (you cannot un-coarse-grain).

III. Fixed Points and Universality

Under repeated RG transformations, the coupling constants flow in theory space.

The flow has special points:

Fixed points:

\[\mathcal{R}_b: g^* o g^*\]

At a fixed point, the physics is scale-invariant — it looks the same at all scales.

There are two types:

UV fixed point (ultraviolet): The theory flows toward this point as you zoom in (to shorter distances). The theory at the UV fixed point describes the fundamental short-distance physics.

IR fixed point (infrared): The theory flows toward this point as you zoom out (to longer distances). The theory at the IR fixed point describes the long-distance, low-energy behavior.

The Gaussian fixed point: The free field theory — no interactions. Many theories flow to this in the IR. This is why perturbation theory works: near the Gaussian fixed point, interactions are small.

Relevant, marginal, and irrelevant operators:

Near a fixed point, perturb the theory by an operator \(\mathcal{O}_i\) with coupling \(g_i\):

Under RG: \(g_i o b^{y_i} g_i\)

  • \(y_i > 0\): relevant — coupling grows under RG. Becomes important at long distances.
  • \(y_i = 0\): marginal — coupling unchanged (at linear order). Need higher-order analysis.
  • \(y_i < 0\): irrelevant — coupling shrinks under RG. Becomes unimportant at long distances.

Universality:

Near an IR fixed point, only relevant operators matter. Irrelevant operators vanish.

This explains universality — why different microscopic systems show identical large-scale behavior:

Different systems with different microscopic details flow to the same IR fixed point.

The Ising model, the liquid-gas transition, certain polymer systems — all belong to the same universality class.

Their critical exponents are identical: \( u = 0.6301..., \quad \eta = 0.0364..., \quad eta = 0.3265...\)

Not approximately identical. Identical to many decimal places.

Why? Because near the critical point, only a handful of relevant operators matter — the microscopic details are irrelevant in the technical RG sense.

The universality class is determined by the symmetry and dimensionality — not by the atomic details.

IV. Critical Phenomena and Scaling

At the critical point — the fixed point of the RG — the system is scale-invariant.

This produces power law behavior:

\[\xi \sim |T - T_c|^{- u}\]

The correlation length \(\xi\) diverges as \(T o T_c\).

All other thermodynamic quantities exhibit power laws near \(T_c\):

$$m \sim T - T_c ^ eta \quad ext{(magnetization)}$$
$$\chi \sim T - T_c ^{-\gamma} \quad ext{(susceptibility)}$$
$$C \sim T - T_c ^{-\alpha} \quad ext{(specific heat)}$$

The critical exponents \( u, eta, \gamma, \alpha, \delta, \eta\) are universal — same for all systems in the universality class.

They satisfy scaling relations:

\(\alpha + 2 eta + \gamma = 2 \quad ext{(Rushbrooke)}\) \(\gamma = u(2 - \eta) \quad ext{(Fisher)}\) \( u d = 2 - \alpha \quad ext{(hyperscaling)}\)

These relations are not empirical. They follow from RG scaling — from the assumption of a fixed point.

Wilson computed the critical exponents using the \(\epsilon\)-expansion: expand around \(d = 4 - \epsilon\) dimensions, compute perturbatively in \(\epsilon\), set \(\epsilon = 1\) for \(d = 3\).

The results agreed with experiments to extraordinary precision.

For the first time, the universal critical exponents were computed from first principles.

V. The RG in Quantum Field Theory

In QFT, the renormalization group takes a different but equivalent form.

The Callan-Symanzik equation:

Physical observables are independent of the arbitrary renormalization scale \(\mu\).

\[\mu rac{d}{d\mu} \mathcal{G}^{(n)} = 0\]

This gives:

\[\left[\mu rac{\partial}{\partial\mu} + eta(g) rac{\partial}{\partial g} - n\gamma(g) ight]\mathcal{G}^{(n)} = 0\]

The beta function \( eta(g)\):

\[ eta(g) = \mu rac{dg}{d\mu}\]

describes how the coupling constant changes with the renormalization scale.

  • \( eta(g) > 0\): coupling increases with energy (IR free, UV divergent)
  • \( eta(g) < 0\): coupling decreases with energy (asymptotic freedom)
  • \( eta(g^*) = 0\): fixed point

Asymptotic freedom:

In QCD (the theory of strong interactions), the beta function is:

\[ eta(g_s) = - rac{g_s^3}{16\pi^2} \left( rac{11}{3}N_c - rac{2}{3}N_f ight)\]

For \(N_c = 3\) colors, \(N_f = 6\) flavors:

\[ eta(g_s) = - rac{7g_s^3}{16\pi^2} < 0\]

The strong coupling decreases at high energies.

At short distances (high energies): quarks are free. At long distances (low energies): quarks are confined.

This is asymptotic freedom — discovered by Gross, Politzer, and Wilczek in 1973 (Nobel Prize 2004).

It explains why perturbation theory works at high energies for QCD but not at low energies — the coupling is small at high energies, large at low energies.

The running of the coupling:

\[\alpha_s(\mu) = rac{\alpha_s(\mu_0)} {1 + rac{\alpha_s(\mu_0)}{2\pi} \left( rac{11}{3}N_c - rac{2}{3}N_f ight) \ln(\mu/\mu_0)}\]

At \(\mu = M_Z = 91.2\) GeV: \(\alpha_s \approx 0.118\) At \(\mu = 1\) GeV: \(\alpha_s \approx 0.5\)

The same coupling — running with scale.

VI. The Effective Field Theory Program

Wilson’s RG framework provides the conceptual foundation for effective field theory (EFT) — the most powerful tool in modern physics.

The idea:

At any given energy scale \(E\), physics is described by an effective Lagrangian containing all operators consistent with the symmetries, organized by their dimension:

\[\mathcal{L}_{eff} = \sum_i rac{c_i}{\Lambda^{d_i - 4}}\mathcal{O}_i\]

where \(\Lambda\) is the cutoff scale, \(d_i\) is the dimension of operator \(\mathcal{O}_i\), and \(c_i\) are Wilson coefficients.

Operators with \(d_i > 4\) are suppressed by powers of \(E/\Lambda\).

At energies \(E \ll \Lambda\): only \(d \leq 4\) operators matter. The EFT is renormalizable.

At energies \(E \sim \Lambda\): higher-dimension operators become important. New physics appears.

The Standard Model as an EFT:

The Standard Model — which we thought was a fundamental theory — is an effective field theory.

It is valid at energies below some cutoff \(\Lambda_{NP}\) — the scale of new physics.

The fact that the SM is renormalizable — contains only \(d \leq 4\) operators — is not a fundamental requirement.

It is an automatic consequence of the EFT framework: at low energies, higher-dimension operators decouple.

The SM is not fundamental. It is the IR description of some UV theory we have not yet found.

Gravity as an EFT:

Even General Relativity can be treated as an EFT — expanded in powers of \(E/m_P\):

\[\mathcal{L}_{gravity} = rac{m_P^2}{2}R + c_1 R^2 + c_2 R_{\mu u}R^{\mu u} + rac{c_3}{m_P^2}R^3 + ...\]

At energies \(E \ll m_P\): only the Einstein-Hilbert term matters. GR is a valid effective theory.

At energies \(E \sim m_P\): all higher-order terms matter. Quantum gravity is necessary.

This is why we can do reliable calculations in quantum gravity at low energies — GR as an EFT gives well-defined predictions — without needing a complete theory of quantum gravity.

VII. The Hierarchy Problem — Where RG Hurts

The renormalization group also reveals one of the deepest problems in physics.

The Higgs mass problem:

The Higgs boson mass receives quantum corrections:

\[m_H^2 = (m_H^{bare})^2 + \delta m_H^2\]

where:

\[\delta m_H^2 \sim rac{3\Lambda^2}{8\pi^2 v^2} \left(m_t^2 - rac{m_W^2}{2} - rac{m_Z^2}{4} - rac{m_H^2}{4} ight)\]

The correction scales as \(\Lambda^2\) — quadratically with the UV cutoff.

If \(\Lambda = m_P \approx 10^{19}\) GeV:

\[\delta m_H^2 \sim \left( rac{10^{19}}{125} ight)^2 m_H^2 \sim 10^{34} m_H^2\]

The bare mass must cancel this to 34 decimal places to give the observed Higgs mass of 125 GeV.

This is the hierarchy problem — or the naturalness problem.

The Higgs mass is quadratically sensitive to UV physics. To get a light Higgs at 125 GeV, the bare parameters must be fine-tuned to extraordinary precision.

Fine-tuning of this magnitude seems unnatural — it suggests we are missing something.

Proposed solutions:

Supersymmetry: Fermion loops cancel boson loops. The quadratic divergence cancels:

\[\delta m_H^2|_{SUSY} \sim m_{SUSY}^2 \ln(\Lambda/m_{SUSY})\]

Logarithmic instead of quadratic. Natural if \(m_{SUSY} \sim ext{TeV}\).

LHC found no SUSY at the TeV scale. Either SUSY is at higher mass — less natural — or SUSY does not solve the hierarchy problem.

Compositeness / Little Higgs: The Higgs is a composite particle — a pseudo-Goldstone boson — at a scale \(\sim 1\) TeV. The quadratic divergence is cut off at the compositeness scale.

No evidence found at LHC.

Extra dimensions: The Planck scale in 4D is large because gravity spreads into extra dimensions. The true Planck scale is \(\sim\) TeV. No hierarchy.

No evidence found.

Anthropic selection: The Higgs mass is fine-tuned because only in universes with light Higgs bosons does complex chemistry — and life — exist.

We cannot falsify this. Many physicists find it unsatisfying.

The RG perspective on naturalness:

The hierarchy problem is a statement about the RG flow.

The Higgs mass operator is relevant at the UV scale — it grows as you flow to lower energies. To keep it small in the IR, you need precise cancellations in the UV.

A natural theory is one where all IR parameters are determined by UV parameters without fine-tuning — where the relevant operators are either absent or protected by symmetry.

The Standard Model is not natural in this sense.

This is not merely aesthetic. It is a strong hint that the SM is incomplete — that new physics exists at some scale that protects the Higgs mass.

We have not found it.

VIII. The Deep Message

The renormalization group tells us something profound about the structure of reality.

Reality is organized by scale.

At each scale, there is an effective description — a set of relevant degrees of freedom and their interactions.

The description at each scale is largely autonomous — insensitive to the details of shorter scales.

This is why science is possible.

If physics at the human scale depended sensitively on the positions of every quark in every proton — if there were no decoupling of scales — no effective theory would exist at any scale.

Everything would depend on everything else. No prediction would be possible. Science would be impossible.

The decoupling of scales — the organization of nature into largely independent effective theories — is the precondition for scientific knowledge.

The RG explains this decoupling.

And it tells us more:

The fundamental theory — whatever UV fixed point the RG flows from — need not be known to do physics at low energies.

The IR physics is determined by the universality class — by the symmetries and the dimensionality — not by the microscopic details.

This is either: (a) a deep truth about reality — nature is organized so that knowledge at each scale does not require knowledge of all scales (b) a feature of the RG mathematical structure — a property of the equations we use to describe nature (c) an anthropic observation — only in a universe with scale decoupling would observers exist to notice it

Probably all three.

IX. Fixed Points Are the Fundamental Objects

I want to make a claim that is not universally shared but that I believe is correct:

The fixed points of the RG — the scale-invariant theories — are the fundamental objects in physics.

Not the specific theories at specific scales. The fixed points.

The Gaussian fixed point — free field theory. The Wilson-Fisher fixed point — critical phenomena. The UV fixed points of asymptotically free theories.

These are the organizing structures around which physics at all scales is organized.

The RG flow connects them: physics flows from UV fixed points (short-distance physics) to IR fixed points (long-distance physics).

The theories we observe at any given scale are points on these flows — transient descriptions, valid over a range of scales, connecting the UV structure to the IR structure.

The fixed points are eternal. The theories between them are approximate.

This is the deepest message of the RG:

Nature is not described by a single theory at a single scale.

It is described by a web of effective theories, organized by their RG flows, anchored at fixed points.

The quest for a “Theory of Everything” is the quest for the UV fixed point — the theory from which all others flow.

We have not found it.

But the RG tells us what we are looking for.

Essential reading:

Wilson & Kogut — “The Renormalization Group and the \(\epsilon\) Expansion” (1974) Phys. Rep. 12, 75 — the foundational paper; dense but essential

Wilson — “The Renormalization Group and Critical Phenomena” (1982) Rev. Mod. Phys. 55, 583 — Wilson’s Nobel lecture; readable

Goldenfeld — Lectures on Phase Transitions and the Renormalization Group (1992) — the best pedagogical treatment

Peskin & Schroeder — An Introduction to QFT — Chapters 12-13 for the QFT RG

Polchinski — “Renormalization and Effective Lagrangians” (1984) Nucl. Phys. B 231, 269 — the modern Wilsonian perspective on QFT

For the philosophically inclined:

Batterman — The Devil in the Details (2002) — on universality and explanation
entry.summary: Why physics at different scales decouples, how universality emerges from microscopic chaos, and why the RG is not a technique but a way of thinking about reality.

entry.title: New York — Notes From Several Visits, Part II

link.title: New York — Notes From Several Visits, Part II

entry.content:
Part I was museums and parks.

Part II is everything else.

I. Mornings at Bryant Park

Before anything else: the morning ritual.

Wake up. Walk to Bryant Park. Stop at Pret a Manger on the way.

[photo: Pret a Manger or morning coffee]

Pret a Manger does not need defending. The sandwiches are fresh, the coffee is good, and it is right there, between the hotel and the park.

Take the sandwich and the coffee into Bryant Park. Find a chair — or better, the grass. Lie down. Look up at the buildings and the sky between them.

[photo: Bryant Park morning]

New York mornings in Bryant Park are genuinely one of the better things available to a person in this world.

The city is already moving — it is always already moving — but the park holds a small pocket of stillness. People reading, people on their phones, pigeons doing pigeon things, office workers cutting through.

I stayed longer than I planned every time.

Bryant Park in the evening:

Bryant Park also does movie nights in summer — free outdoor screenings on the lawn, films projected on a large screen with the Midtown skyline behind it.

[photo: Bryant Park movie night if you have one]

Hundreds of people on blankets and chairs. Classic films mostly. The city as a backdrop.

I have not managed to time a visit to catch one of these. This is on the list.

Bryant Park also hosts concerts — free, regular, across genres. I caught part of one once, standing at the edge of the park with my coffee, not planning to stay, staying anyway.

II. The Bookstores

New York has bookstores the way other cities have coffee shops — everywhere, varied, each with its own character.

I visited as many as I could.

The Strand

The Strand is the one everyone knows. 18 miles of books, the sign says. I believe it.

[photo: Strand exterior or interior]

The Strand is overwhelming in the best way. Four floors, every section, new and used, rare books upstairs in glass cases, staff recommendation shelves that are genuinely worth reading.

I have a rule at the Strand: I go in with a budget. I always spend more than the budget. This is fine.

The rare books room upstairs — first editions, signed copies, books that are themselves objects — is worth the trip even if you buy nothing.

I looked at a signed first edition of a Hemingway novel for a long time. I did not buy it. I thought about it for the rest of the day.

[photo: Strand interior, shelves]

Other bookstores:

New York has independent bookstores in almost every neighborhood — each one reflecting its neighborhood, its owners, its particular idea of what a bookstore should be.

I wandered into several without planning to. This is the correct approach to New York bookstores.

[photo: any independent bookstore]

The rule: if you walk past a bookstore and something in the window catches your eye, you go in. You always go in.

III. Chinatown and Little Italy

They are next to each other — or rather, Chinatown has been slowly absorbing Little Italy for decades, which is either a shame or just how neighborhoods work.

Both are still there. Both are worth an afternoon.

Chinatown:

[photo: Chinatown street]

Dense, loud, completely itself. Canal Street with its market stalls, the fish markets with tanks in the windows, the bakeries with pork buns and egg tarts, the tea shops, the vegetable vendors.

I ate a pork bun standing on the street. This is mandatory.

[photo: pork bun or Chinatown food]

Chinatown does not perform itself for tourists. It is just what it is — a neighborhood where people live and work, that happens to have extraordinary food available on every corner.

Little Italy:

[photo: Little Italy street]

Smaller than it used to be — Mulberry Street is the main artery now, a few blocks of restaurants and cafés with red and green decorations and menus in the windows.

It is slightly touristy. It is also genuinely good.

I had a cannoli from a bakery on Mulberry Street that was the correct size, filled to order, dusted with powdered sugar.

[photo: cannoli or Little Italy café]

I ate it walking down the street. Also mandatory.

IV. SoHo

SoHo is expensive now — expensive shops, expensive restaurants, expensive coffee in cups that have been designed very carefully.

It is also beautiful.

[photo: SoHo cast iron buildings]

The cast iron architecture — 19th century buildings with elaborate iron facades, wide windows, the particular light that comes through those wide windows in the afternoon —

is some of the most distinctive streetscape in America.

Walk down Greene Street or Broome Street on a good afternoon and the buildings do something to your sense of what a city can look like.

The shops are too expensive. I looked in windows. This is free.

[photo: SoHo street or window display]

SoHo also has good galleries — smaller than the major museums, more varied, more willing to show work that hasn’t been canonized yet.

I walked into two or three. Stayed in one for a while. This is the other correct approach to New York galleries: walk in without a plan, leave when you’re ready.

V. New Yorkers

A note on the people.

New Yorkers walk fast. This is not rudeness — it is physics. Eight million people in a small space, all of them somewhere to be.

[photo: busy New York street]

The first time I was in New York I kept getting in the way. I was walking at a normal human pace and people were flowing around me the way water flows around a rock — efficiently, without acknowledgment.

Now I walk faster. I have learned.

New Yorkers are not actually rude. They are efficient. There is a difference.

Ask someone for directions — genuinely, clearly — and they will stop, give you very specific directions, possibly walk you partway there if you seem lost enough.

They will not make small talk while doing it. This is also fine.

Seinfeld and Friends:

I grew up watching both shows.

Seinfeld’s New York: the Upper West Side, Monk’s Cafe, Jerry’s apartment on West 81st Street, the specific rhythm of four people whose entire lives happen within a few city blocks. The show is famously about nothing — and the nothing is entirely New York: the diner conversations, the street arguments, the elaborate social contracts of people living too close together.

[photo: Upper West Side street, or just a New York diner]

Friends’ New York: less accurate, more aspirational — those apartments do not exist on those salaries, that couch in Central Perk is always available, the city is somehow both enormous and a small village where everyone keeps running into everyone.

But Friends got something right too: the energy of being young in New York, the feeling that the city is a character in whatever story you are living, that around any corner something interesting might be happening.

[photo: Central Park or coffee shop]

The first time I walked through the Upper West Side, I recognized it. Not because I had been there — but because I had seen it a thousand times on a screen.

The buildings were the right height. The streets were the right width. The delis had the right signs in the windows.

New York was the first city I visited that I already knew before I arrived.

That is what decades of television will do.

The city delivered on both shows — the efficiency of Seinfeld’s New York and the energy of Friends’ New York. Slightly more Seinfeld than Friends, if I’m being honest.

The apartments are smaller. The couch at Central Perk is never free.

[photo: New York street life, people]

VI. Broadway — The Unfinished Item

I have not been to Broadway.

This needs to be corrected.

Every visit, I think: this time. Every visit, something else fills the days — another museum, another neighborhood, another afternoon in Bryant Park that stretches longer than planned.

Broadway remains on the list.

Hamilton:

Hamilton is the specific thing I want to see.

[photo: Hamilton marquee if you have one, or Richard Rodgers Theatre exterior]

Lin-Manuel Miranda’s musical about Alexander Hamilton — told through hip-hop, R&B, and show tunes, with a cast that reflects the America Hamilton’s generation was building but could not yet imagine.

I have listened to the cast recording more times than I will admit here.

I know most of the lyrics.

I have not seen it live.

This is an ongoing situation that will be resolved on the next visit.

Or the one after that. But it will be resolved.

The Richard Rodgers Theatre on 46th Street — I have walked past it. I looked at the marquee. I did not go in because I had not planned ahead and the tickets are expensive and I told myself next time.

Next time.

VII. The List That Doesn’t Finish

Every visit to New York adds more to the list than it removes.

Done: The Met. MoMA. The Strand. Bryant Park mornings. Lobster rolls. Chinatown pork buns. Little Italy cannoli. Filaga pizza. The High Line. The NYPL Treasures. SoHo streets. Refik Anadol. Basquiat. Van Gogh twice.

Still on the list: Hamilton. Bryant Park movie night. The Frick Collection. The Whitney. A proper diner breakfast — eggs, coffee, the works. The Brooklyn Botanic Garden in spring. Coney Island, once, just to say I went. A full day in the West Village with no plan at all. Jazz somewhere small and dark on a weeknight. The Tenement Museum on the Lower East Side. One full evening just walking without any destination.

New York is the only city I know where the list grows faster than you can walk.

This is not a problem. It is the point.

[photo: New York at night, or street, or whatever feels right]

New York, multiple visits. The list continues.
entry.summary: Bookstores, Strand, Little Italy, Chinatown, SoHo, Bryant Park movie nights, Pret a Manger mornings, and Broadway — the list that never finishes.

entry.title: Seneca — On Time, Death, and the Art of Living Before You Die

link.title: Seneca — On Time, Death, and the Art of Living Before You Die

entry.content:
“Omnia, Lucili, aliena sunt, tempus tantum nostrum est.”

Everything, Lucilius, belongs to others. Time alone is ours.

Seneca wrote this in a letter. He was an old man. He had been one of the most powerful men in Rome — tutor and advisor to Nero, the most feared emperor in history. He had been extraordinarily wealthy. He had been exiled, recalled, celebrated, suspected.

And he sat down and wrote to his friend Lucilius: time alone is ours.

Not the palace. Not the money. Not the influence. Not the books or the reputation or the name.

Time.

And then, immediately, the devastating follow-up:

“Yet such is the ebb and flow of fortune that we allow time — the one thing that is truly ours — to be taken from us.”

I. The Man

Lucius Annaeus Seneca was born around 4 BCE in Córdoba, in Roman Spain.

He came to Rome as a child, studied rhetoric and philosophy, became a brilliant advocate and writer, and rose to prominence under the emperor Claudius.

Then Claudius’s wife accused him of adultery with the emperor’s niece. He was exiled to Corsica for eight years.

He was recalled by Agrippina — Claudius’s new wife, Nero’s mother — to serve as tutor to her son.

When Nero became emperor at sixteen, Seneca was his most important advisor. For several years, the partnership worked — Seneca’s moderation restraining the emperor’s cruelty.

Then it didn’t.

Nero became what Nero became. Seneca retreated from public life — tried to give back his wealth, tried to disengage from the court.

In 65 CE, Nero accused him of involvement in the Pisonian conspiracy — a plot to assassinate the emperor. The evidence was thin. It didn’t matter.

Nero sent soldiers to Seneca’s villa with an order to die.

Seneca opened his veins.

He died slowly — the veins of an old man do not bleed quickly. He took poison; it didn’t work. He was placed in a hot bath to hasten the bleeding.

He dictated to scribes until he could no longer speak.

He died as he had written — discussing philosophy in the face of death, practicing what he had preached in letter after letter, essay after essay, for thirty years.

II. On the Shortness of Life

De Brevitate Vitae — On the Shortness of Life — is perhaps the most important thing Seneca wrote.

It begins with a paradox:

“It is not that we have a short time to live, but that we waste a great deal of it.”

Life is not short. Life is long enough — more than long enough — if used well.

The problem is not the quantity of time. It is what we do with it.

We give our time to everyone who asks. We guard our money carefully — we are appalled if someone wastes our property — but we let people waste our hours, our days, our years, without protest, without even noticing.

“People are frugal in guarding their personal property; but as soon as it comes to squandering time they are most wasteful of the one thing in which it is right to be stingy.”

Seneca identifies the thieves of time:

The ambitious — always preparing to live, always arranging the conditions under which life will begin. First I need this promotion. Then this house. Then this security. Then I will begin to live.

The pleasure-seekers — always consuming, never present, always moving to the next experience.

The distracted — pulled in every direction by other people’s urgencies, never attending to their own.

The greedy — spending their life accumulating what life cannot be lived without spending.

All of them, Seneca says, are dying badly — not because death is near but because they are not living.

“They lose the day in expectation of the night, and the night in fear of the dawn.”

III. The Letters to Lucilius

The Epistulae Morales — Moral Letters to Lucilius — are Seneca’s masterpiece.

One hundred and twenty-four letters, written in the last years of his life, to his friend Gaius Lucilius Junior — a man younger than Seneca, a government official in Sicily, who had apparently asked Seneca to help him live better.

The letters are not treatises. They are not systematic philosophy. They are the most intimate philosophical document from the ancient world — a man thinking out loud, revising himself, admitting uncertainty, sharing what he has learned and what he has failed to learn.

They begin, famously:

“Ita fac, mi Lucili: vindica te tibi.”

Do this, my Lucilius: claim yourself for yourself.

The first letter. The first sentence. Already everything is there.

Claim yourself for yourself — take back the time that has been dispersed, the attention that has been scattered, the self that has been given away in small pieces to everyone who asked.

Each letter takes up a theme — friendship, travel, the fear of death, anger, grief, luxury, poverty, solitude, crowds — and works it through with a combination of personal reflection, philosophical argument, and the occasional devastating aphorism that lodges in the memory and will not leave.

IV. On Friendship

Seneca’s letters are, above all, about friendship.

Not friendship as a social category — as acquaintance, as usefulness, as pleasant company.

Friendship as the deepest form of human connection — the relationship in which another person is genuinely known and genuinely valued.

“Hoc primum philosophia promittit: sensum communem, humanitatem et congregationem.”

“Philosophy promises above all: common sense, humanity, and fellowship.”

And more directly:

“Sine amico nemo tutus vivere potest.”

“Without a friend, no one can live securely.”

But Seneca is precise about what friendship requires:

“Before you make a friend, judge whether he is worthy to be made a friend. After you have made a friend, trust him with everything.”

The order matters. Judgment first. Then total trust. There is no friendship without judgment — the assessment of character, of values, of reliability. And there is no friendship with only judgment — the trust must be complete, or it is not friendship.

He also says something that feels almost modern in its psychological precision:

“Recede in te ipse quantum potes.”

“Retire into yourself as much as you can.”

Solitude and friendship are not opposites for Seneca. They are complementary disciplines. You must know yourself before you can truly know another. And you must know another to truly know yourself.

The friend is a mirror — not a flattering one, but an honest one.

“Refer everything to a good person who you would want to imitate. Live as if he is watching.”

V. On Death

Seneca returns to death in almost every letter.

Not morbidly — not with the fascination of depression — but with the insistence of a man who has understood that the refusal to think about death is the primary source of the wasted life.

“Omnia, Lucili, aliena sunt, tempus tantum nostrum est.”

Time is ours — and it runs out. To not know this — to live as if you will not die — is to be unable to choose.

You cannot choose well what to do with your life if you do not know how much of it remains. You cannot prioritize correctly if you believe there will always be more time.

“Cogita quantum temporis absumpserit.”

“Think how much time has already been consumed.”

Not to produce guilt — but to produce clarity.

The Stoic exercise of memento mori — remember that you will die — is not pessimism. It is the most practical philosophical tool Seneca describes: the practice of holding death in mind as the clarifying horizon against which all choices become legible.

What matters, if today were the last day? What would you stop doing? What would you start?

“Dum differtur vita transcurrit.”

“While we are postponing, life speeds by.”

And his most personal formulation — written, perhaps, when the soldiers were already on their way:

“Omnia, Lucili, aliena sunt, tempus tantum nostrum est. In hoc unum avarus esse licet.”

“Everything belongs to others, Lucilius; time alone is ours. In this one thing it is permitted to be a miser.”

Be a miser with time. Give it to no one carelessly. Spend it on nothing that does not deserve it.

VI. The Stoic Framework

Seneca was a Stoic — part of the philosophical school founded by Zeno of Citium in Athens around 300 BCE.

But he was an unusual Stoic — the most human of them, the most willing to admit difficulty, the most honest about the gap between what philosophy teaches and what a person actually does.

The Stoic framework:

The dichotomy of control: Some things are up to us — our judgments, desires, aversions. Some things are not — our body, reputation, wealth, other people.

Unhappiness comes from wanting what is not up to us. Freedom comes from wanting only what is up to us.

The discipline of desire: Desire only what virtue requires. Prefer — but do not require — health, wealth, friendship. Be indifferent — in the technical sense — to everything external.

Living according to nature: The rational animal’s nature is reason. To live well is to live rationally — in accordance with the logos, the rational principle that structures reality.

Seneca accepted all of this. He also accepted that he often failed to live it.

This honesty is what distinguishes him from the more austere Stoics — Epictetus, who was enslaved and never complained, Marcus Aurelius, who ruled an empire and wrote his private doubts in a notebook.

Seneca was wealthy, powerful, sometimes hypocritical — he wrote about the dangers of wealth while being extraordinarily rich. He wrote about the importance of not fearing death while apparently fearing it.

His critics — then and now — point this out.

His response, in one of the letters:

“I am not a wise man, nor shall I ever be one. Demand of me not that I be equal to the best, but that I be better than the wicked. I am satisfied if I subtract something from my vices each day.”

Not perfection. Improvement. Not the ideal Stoic sage — a figure even the Stoics admitted was theoretical. A man trying, imperfectly, daily, to do better.

This is what makes him readable across two thousand years.

VII. The Consolations

Seneca wrote three formal consolations — letters to people in grief — that are among the most moving prose of the ancient world.

To Marcia — a woman who had lost her son. To Helvia — his own mother, written during his exile. To Polybius — a freedman at Claudius’s court, who had lost his brother.

The Ad Helviam — to his mother — is extraordinary.

He has been exiled. He is in Corsica — far from Rome, far from his work, far from his friends. And he writes to his mother not to receive consolation but to give it.

“I have decided, dearest mother, to conquer my grief rather than to cheat it.”

He does not pretend the exile doesn’t hurt. He does not perform Stoic indifference. He acknowledges the pain — his pain, her pain — and then works, carefully, philosophically, to find the resources within that pain for endurance.

The resources he finds: the constancy of virtue, which cannot be exiled. The life of the mind, which no location can confine. The friendship of philosophy, which travels everywhere. The love between mother and son, which distance cannot touch.

“Wherever I turn, I see the proofs of your affection.”

This is Stoicism with a human face — not the denial of feeling but its transformation through honest philosophical attention.

VIII. Seneca and Power

The most difficult question about Seneca is the one his critics have always asked:

How do you square the philosophy with the life?

He wrote about the dangers of wealth — and was fabulously wealthy. He wrote about the importance of virtue — and served a murderer. He wrote about the shortness of life — and spent much of it in the service of Nero’s court.

Did he know what Nero was? Almost certainly. Did he stay because he believed he could moderate the evil? Perhaps. For a time. Did he stay too long? Almost certainly.

The charge of hypocrisy is serious. Philosophy that cannot be lived is not philosophy — it is literature.

Seneca’s answer, from the letters:

“Do not look at who says this — look at what is said.”

The teaching stands independent of the teacher. The truth of omnia aliena sunt, tempus tantum nostrum est does not depend on whether Seneca spent his own time well.

This is both a defense and an evasion. It is also, in a certain limited sense, right.

The letters are true whether Seneca lived them or not. But they would be truer if he had. And we should hold both truths simultaneously — the wisdom of the letters and the failure of the life — without letting either cancel the other.

This is what it means to read a philosopher honestly.

IX. The Death

When the soldiers came, Seneca was ready.

Or as ready as a man can be who has spent decades preparing and still, in the end, is a body that bleeds slowly.

He tried to comfort his wife, Paulina, who wanted to die with him. He asked her to live.

“I showed you ways of looking after life; you prefer the glory of dying. I will not begrudge you such a noble example. Let the fortitude of this our common end be alike — but let there be more in your departure that is worthy of praise.”

She survived. Nero ordered it. She lived many years after. The scars on her wrists marked her for the rest of her life.

Seneca dictated his last words. We do not know exactly what they were — Tacitus says they were spoken to friends and scribes — but he died speaking.

Still thinking. Still in the letters.

X. What He Gives Us

Seneca gives us something that most philosophers do not:

The permission to be imperfect while still trying.

Not the Platonic philosopher-king who has seen the sun. Not the Zen master who has achieved emptiness. Not the perfect Stoic sage who is indifferent to everything external.

A man. Brilliant, flawed, compromised, trying. Writing letters to a friend about how to live better.

“Nusquam est qui ubique est.”

“One who is everywhere is nowhere.”

“Recede in te ipse quantum potes.”

“Retire into yourself as much as you can.”

“Ita fac, mi Lucili: vindica te tibi.”

“Claim yourself for yourself.”

Two thousand years old. Written this morning. Written for you.

Further reading:

Seneca — Letters from a Stoic (trans. Robin Campbell — Penguin Classics)

Seneca — On the Shortness of Life (trans. C.D.N. Costa)

Seneca — Dialogues and Essays (trans. John Davie — Oxford World’s Classics)

For context: Paul Veyne — Seneca: The Life of a Stoic (2003)

For the Stoic framework: Marcus Aurelius — Meditations (trans. Gregory Hays)
entry.summary: The most human of the Stoics — on wasted time, genuine friendship, and why we are all dying badly.

entry.title: Logical Operators, Code Degeneracy, and the Geometry of Quantum Codes

link.title: Logical Operators, Code Degeneracy, and the Geometry of Quantum Codes

entry.content:
The previous essay introduced quantum error correction through the stabilizer formalism. https://gulfiliz.github.io/blog/2026/qec/

It described what stabilizer codes do.

This essay describes what they are — the mathematical structure underneath: the symplectic geometry of Pauli space, the precise definition of logical operators, the meaning of code degeneracy, and why finding high-distance codes is a hard combinatorial problem.

This is the mathematics that a working quantum error correction researcher lives in daily.

I. The Symplectic Representation

Every Pauli operator on \(n\) qubits can be represented as a binary vector in \(\mathbb{F}_2^{2n}\).

The representation:

\[P = i^k X^{a_1}Z^{b_1} \otimes \cdots \otimes X^{a_n}Z^{b_n} \leftrightarrow \mathbf{v} = (a_1,...,a_n \mid b_1,...,b_n) \in \mathbb{F}_2^{2n}\]

Ignoring phases (which are irrelevant for commutation relations): each Pauli operator corresponds to a binary vector of length \(2n\).

The X-part: first \(n\) bits. The Z-part: last \(n\) bits.

The symplectic inner product:

Two Pauli operators commute or anticommute depending on their symplectic inner product:

\[\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} angle_s = \mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}' + \mathbf{b} \cdot \mathbf{a}' \pmod{2}\]

where \(\mathbf{u} = (\mathbf{a} \mid \mathbf{b})\) and \(\mathbf{v} = (\mathbf{a}' \mid \mathbf{b}')\).

\(\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} angle_s = 0\): operators commute.

\(\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} angle_s = 1\): operators anticommute.

The symplectic form:

In matrix notation:

\[\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} angle_s = \mathbf{u} \Omega \mathbf{v}^T\]

where:

\[\Omega = egin{pmatrix} 0 & I_n \ I_n & 0 \end{pmatrix}\]

This is a symplectic form on \(\mathbb{F}_2^{2n}\) — the space \((\mathbb{F}_2^{2n}, \Omega)\) is a symplectic vector space.

The entire theory of stabilizer codes lives in this symplectic geometry.

II. The Stabilizer Code as a Subspace

A stabilizer code with \(m\) independent generators corresponds to an \(m imes 2n\) binary matrix \(H\) — the rows are the symplectic representations of the generators.

The isotropic condition:

The stabilizer group is abelian — all generators commute. In the symplectic representation:

\[H \Omega H^T = 0 \pmod{2}\]

This is the isotropic condition — the rowspace of \(H\) is an isotropic subspace of $$(\mathbb{F}_2^{2n}, \Omega)$.

An isotropic subspace has the property that every pair of vectors in it has zero symplectic inner product.

This is the fundamental algebraic condition on a stabilizer code.

The code space:

The rowspace of \(H\):

\[S = ext{rowspace}(H) \subseteq \mathbb{F}_2^{2n}\]

with \(|S| = 2^m\) elements (as a set of vectors) and \(\dim(S) = m\).

The code space \(\mathcal{C}\) is the \(+1\) eigenspace of all stabilizers — a \(2^{n-m}\)-dimensional subspace of the \(2^n\)-dimensional Hilbert space.

The number of encoded logical qubits:

\[k = n - m\]

when the \(m\) generators are independent.

The rank of H:

The effective number of independent stabilizers is \( ext{rank}(H)\) over \(\mathbb{F}_2\).

If the generators are not independent — if some generator is the product of others — then \( ext{rank}(H) < m\) and the code encodes more logical qubits:

\[k = n - ext{rank}(H)\]

This is why computing \(k\) for a given polynomial pair in BB codes requires explicit rank computation — not just counting generators.

III. The Normalizer and Logical Operators

The stabilizer \(S\) has a centralizer in the Pauli group: the set of all Pauli operators that commute with every element of \(S\).

In the symplectic representation, this is the symplectic complement of \(S\):

\[S^\perp = \{\mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{F}_2^{2n} : \langle \mathbf{s}, \mathbf{v} angle_s = 0 \; orall \mathbf{s} \in S\}\] \[= ext{rowspace}(H\Omega)^\perp\]

Explicitly:

\[S^\perp = \ker(H\Omega) \pmod{2}\]

The key inclusion:

\[S \subseteq S^\perp\]

This follows from the isotropic condition: every stabilizer commutes with every stabilizer.

The quotient space:

\[S^\perp / S\]

has dimension:

\[\dim(S^\perp) - \dim(S) = (n + k) - (n - k) = 2k\]

Wait — let me be precise.

\(\dim(S) = n - k\) \(\dim(S^\perp) = 2n - (n-k) = n + k\)

(since \(S^\perp\) is the symplectic complement of \(S\) in a \(2n\)-dimensional space, and \(\dim(S^\perp) = 2n - \dim(S)\))

\[\dim(S^\perp/S) = (n+k) - (n-k) = 2k\]

Logical operators:

Elements of \(S^\perp \setminus S\) — operators that commute with all stabilizers but are not themselves stabilizers — are the logical operators.

For \(k\) logical qubits, there are \(k\) logical X operators \( ar{X}_1, ..., ar{X}_k\) and \(k\) logical Z operators \( ar{Z}_1, ..., ar{Z}_k\) satisfying:

\[[ ar{X}_i, ar{Z}_j] = 0 ext{ for } i eq j\] \[\{ ar{X}_i, ar{Z}_i\} eq 0 ext{ (they anticommute)}\] \[[ ar{X}_i, S] = 0, \quad [ ar{Z}_i, S] = 0 ext{ for all stabilizers } S\]

In the symplectic picture: the \(2k\)-dimensional space \(S^\perp/S\) carries a symplectic structure inherited from \(\Omega\) — the logical operators form a symplectic basis of this quotient space.

Non-uniqueness of logical operators:

The logical operators are not unique. If \( ar{X}\) is a logical X operator, then \( ar{X} \cdot S\) for any stabilizer \(S\) is an equally valid logical X.

The physical operator representing a logical operation is defined only up to multiplication by stabilizers.

This is crucial for decoding: the decoder does not need to identify the exact error — it only needs to identify the error up to stabilizer equivalence.

IV. Code Distance — The Geometric Picture

The code distance \(d\) is defined as:

\[d = \min \{wt(\mathbf{v}) : \mathbf{v} \in S^\perp \setminus S\}\]

where \(wt(\mathbf{v})\) is the Hamming weight — the number of nonzero positions.

Interpretation:

The minimum-weight logical operator.

If the minimum-weight element of \(S^\perp \setminus S\) has weight \(d\), then any error of weight \(< d/2\) can be uniquely identified and corrected.

An error of weight \(\geq d/2\) may map a codeword to another codeword (after correction) — a logical error.

Separate X and Z distances:

For CSS codes, the distance separates:

\[d_X = \min\{wt(\mathbf{v}) : \mathbf{v} \in C_1 \setminus C_2^\perp\}\] \[d_Z = \min\{wt(\mathbf{v}) : \mathbf{v} \in C_2 \setminus C_1^\perp\}\] \[d = \min(d_X, d_Z)\]

Finding the code distance requires finding the minimum-weight codeword in a coset of a binary linear code — a problem equivalent to minimum-weight codeword in the quotient code.

This is NP-hard in general.

For specific code families — BB codes in particular — special structure can be exploited to compute distance efficiently.

V. Code Degeneracy

A feature of quantum codes with no classical analogue: degeneracy.

Definition:

A code is degenerate if there exists an error \(E otin S\) — not a stabilizer — with the same syndrome as the identity (i.e., \(E \in S^\perp\)) but with weight \(< d\).

Equivalently: there exist elements of \(S^\perp \setminus S\) with weight less than \(d\).

Wait — this would contradict the definition of \(d\).

Let me be more careful.

The precise definition:

A code is degenerate if there exists an error \(E\) with \(wt(E) \leq \lfloor (d-1)/2 floor\) that is in the stabilizer group \(S\) — a low-weight stabilizer.

Equivalently: \(S\) contains elements of weight \(< d\).

In a non-degenerate code: all stabilizers have weight \(\geq d\). Different errors of weight \(< d/2\) produce different syndromes — unique decoding is possible.

In a degenerate code: some stabilizers have weight \(< d\). Errors of weight \(< d/2\) that are stabilizers produce the zero syndrome — they are not detected. But they also do not corrupt the logical state (since they are stabilizers).

The implication for decoding:

In a degenerate code, multiple physical errors can correspond to the same correction — the decoder does not need to identify the exact error, only its equivalence class in \(S^\perp/S\).

This can be advantageous: degenerate codes can potentially achieve better effective thresholds because the stabilizer structure automatically corrects some errors.

BB codes and degeneracy:

BB codes are generically degenerate — the circulant structure of the stabilizer matrix produces low-weight stabilizers (products of generators) that fall within the correction radius.

Quantifying the degeneracy — counting the low-weight stabilizers and their effect on the effective distance — is an active area of research.

VI. The Tanner Graph and Belief Propagation

The structure of a stabilizer code is naturally represented as a Tanner graph — a bipartite graph connecting qubits (variable nodes) to stabilizers (check nodes).

Construction:

Variable nodes: one per qubit, \(n\) total. Check nodes: one per stabilizer generator.

An edge connects qubit \(j\) to stabilizer \(S_i\) if \(S_i\) acts nontrivially on qubit \(j\).

For a CSS code: separate Tanner graphs for X-checks and Z-checks.

The LDPC condition:

A code is LDPC if: — Each variable node has degree \(\leq w_c\) (constant) — Each check node has degree \(\leq w_r\) (constant)

In physical terms: each qubit participates in at most \(w_c\) stabilizers, and each stabilizer acts on at most \(w_r\) qubits.

Cycles in the Tanner graph:

The girth of the Tanner graph — the length of the shortest cycle — is critical for decoding performance.

Belief propagation on a cycle-free graph (a tree) converges exactly to the maximum-likelihood solution.

Short cycles cause BP to oscillate and fail to converge.

For BB codes: the Tanner graph has girth $$\geq 6$ — no cycles of length 4. This is a consequence of the group algebra structure.

Higher girth → better BP performance.

The girth is determined by the specific polynomial pair \((a, b)\) — different polynomial choices give different girth values, affecting decoder performance.

VII. The Polynomial Structure of BB Codes — In Depth

We now examine the algebraic structure of BB codes more carefully.

The group algebra:

\(\mathbb{F}_2[\mathbb{Z}_L imes \mathbb{Z}_M]\) is the group algebra of \(\mathbb{Z}_L imes \mathbb{Z}_M\) over \(\mathbb{F}_2\).

Elements are polynomials \(f(x, y) = \sum_{i,j} f_{ij} x^i y^j\) with \(f_{ij} \in \mathbb{F}_2\) and the relations \(x^L = 1\), \(y^M = 1\).

Multiplication is convolution modulo the group relations.

The circulant structure:

Each element \(f(x,y) \in \mathbb{F}_2[\mathbb{Z}_L imes \mathbb{Z}_M]\) corresponds to an \(LM imes LM\) matrix — a block circulant matrix with circulant blocks.

The matrix is determined by the polynomial \(f\) and the group structure.

The parity check matrices:

For polynomials \(a(x,y)\) and \(b(x,y)\):

\[H_X = [A \mid B], \quad H_Z = [B^T \mid A^T]\]

where \(A\) and \(B\) are the circulant matrices corresponding to \(a\) and \(b\).

The CSS orthogonality condition:

\[H_X H_Z^T = AB^T + BA^T = 0 \pmod{2}\]

In the group algebra: \(ab^* + ba^* = 0\) where \(f^*(x,y) = f(x^{-1}, y^{-1})\) is the “conjugate” polynomial.

The number of logical qubits:

\[k = 2\dim\ker egin{pmatrix}H_X \ H_Z\end{pmatrix} - 2(n - ext{rank}(H_X) - ext{rank}(H_Z))\]

More precisely:

\[k = n - ext{rank}(H_X) - ext{rank}(H_Z) + ext{rank}(H_X \cap \ker H_Z)\]

Wait — the correct formula for a CSS code:

\[k = k_1 + k_2 - n\]

where \(k_1 = n - ext{rank}(H_Z)\) and \(k_2 = n - ext{rank}(H_X)\).

Therefore:

\[k = n - ext{rank}(H_X) - ext{rank}(H_Z)\]

For BB codes with \(n = 2LM\):

\[k = 2(LM - ext{rank}(A)) + 2(LM - ext{rank}(B)) - 2LM\]

No — let me be careful. \(H_X = [A \mid B]\) has dimensions \(LM imes 2LM\). \( ext{rank}(H_X) \leq LM\).

The formula:

\[k = 2LM - ext{rank}(H_X) - ext{rank}(H_Z)\]

Since \(H_X = [A \mid B]\) and \(H_Z = [B^T \mid A^T]\) and both have the same rank by the CSS structure:

\[k = 2(LM - ext{rank}([A \mid B]))\]

The rank of \([A \mid B]\) depends on the specific polynomials \(a, b\) and the group \(\mathbb{Z}_L imes \mathbb{Z}_M\).

Computing the rank:

Over \(\mathbb{F}_2\), with the circulant structure, the rank can be computed using the Smith normal form or the eigenvalue structure of the circulant matrices.

For the group \(\mathbb{Z}_{12} imes \mathbb{Z}_6\):

The group algebra decomposes as:

\[\mathbb{F}_2[\mathbb{Z}_{12} imes \mathbb{Z}_6] \cong \prod_{d_1 | 12, d_2 | 6} \mathbb{F}_2[\mathbb{Z}_{d_1} imes \mathbb{Z}_{d_2}]^{m(d_1,d_2)}\]

where the product is over divisors and \(m(d_1, d_2)\) are multiplicities.

The rank of \([A \mid B]\) decomposes into contributions from each component — allowing efficient rank computation.

This algebraic structure is what makes systematic polynomial search feasible for specific groups.

VIII. Distance Bounds

Upper bounds:

The distance of a code is bounded above by the minimum-weight logical operator.

Finding low-weight logical operators provides upper bounds on \(d\).

For BB codes: the minimum-weight logical operators can be found by brute-force search for small groups, or by algebraic methods exploiting the circulant structure.

Lower bounds:

Proving \(d \geq d_0\) requires showing that no logical operator has weight \(< d_0\).

This is harder — it requires ruling out all low-weight elements of \(S^\perp \setminus S\).

For small codes: exhaustive search. For large codes: probabilistic arguments or structural properties of the code.

The distance of [[72, 12, 7]] codes:

For the specific family of BB codes in \(\mathbb{Z}_{12} imes \mathbb{Z}_6\) with \(n = 144\) physical qubits:

Codes with \(k = 12\) and \(d = 7\) are found by:

  1. Enumerating polynomial pairs \((a, b)\) of weight 3 over \(\mathbb{Z}_{12} imes \mathbb{Z}_6\)
  2. For each pair, computing \(k\) (via rank computation)
  3. For pairs with \(k = 12\), computing \(d\) (via logical operator search)
  4. Keeping pairs with \(d \geq 7\)

Wait — the group order is \(LM = 12 imes 6 = 72\), so \(n = 2 imes 72 = 144\).

But codes with parameters \([[72, 12, 7]]\) have \(n = 72\) — half the standard BB code length.

The resolution: BB codes can also be constructed with \(n = LM\) by using a different matrix structure — or the \([[72, 12, 7]]\) codes come from a group of order 36, not 72.

For \(\mathbb{Z}_{12} imes \mathbb{Z}_6\) with \(LM = 72\):

Standard BB code: \(n = 2 imes 72 = 144\).

The \([[72, 12, 7]]\) codes arise from a different construction — possibly from a subgroup of order 36, or from a modified CSS structure that uses a single circulant rather than the \([A \mid B]\) structure.

The precise construction and the novelty of specific polynomial pairs — their relationship to Shaw & Terhal’s Table VIII constructions — is the active research question.

IX. Automorphisms and Code Equivalence

Two codes are equivalent if one can be obtained from the other by a combination of: — Qubit permutations — Local Clifford operations (single-qubit unitaries) — Qubit relabeling

Equivalent codes have the same parameters \((n, k, d)\) but are “the same code” from a fault-tolerance perspective.

The automorphism group:

The automorphism group \( ext{Aut}(\mathcal{C})\) of a stabilizer code: the set of physical operations that map the code to itself — that permute codewords among codewords.

\[ ext{Aut}(\mathcal{C}) \leq \mathcal{C}_n\]

where \(\mathcal{C}_n\) is the Clifford group.

A larger automorphism group means more structure — more potential transversal gates, more symmetries to exploit.

For BB codes:

The circulant structure of BB codes gives a natural automorphism: the translation symmetry of the group \(\mathbb{Z}_L imes \mathbb{Z}_M\).

Translating all qubits by a group element \(g\): \((A, B) o (g \cdot A, g \cdot B)\)

maps the code to itself — this translation is an automorphism.

Different polynomial pairs may have additional automorphisms beyond the translation symmetry.

The automorphism group determines which logical gates can be implemented transversally — the transversal gate group is exactly the automorphism group (intersected with the logical Clifford group).

Searching for polynomial pairs with large or specific automorphism groups is a way to find codes with useful transversal gate sets.

X. Topological Codes as Geometric Codes

The surface code — and toric code — have a beautiful geometric interpretation that illuminates their distance properties.

The toric code as a chain complex:

The toric code lives on a torus \(T^2\). Qubits on edges. Stabilizers on plaquettes (Z) and vertices (X).

This is a 2D chain complex:

\[\mathbb{F}_2^{|V|} \xrightarrow{\partial_1} \mathbb{F}_2^{|E|} \xrightarrow{\partial_2} \mathbb{F}_2^{|F|}\]

where \(V, E, F\) are vertices, edges, faces.

The boundary maps \(\partial_1, \partial_2\) are the vertex-edge and edge-face incidence matrices.

The stabilizers: \(H_X = \partial_2^T\) (face operators) \(H_Z = \partial_1\) (vertex operators)

The CSS orthogonality: \(H_X H_Z^T = \partial_2^T \partial_1^T = (\partial_1 \partial_2)^T = 0\)

follows from \(\partial_1 \partial_2 = 0\) — the fundamental identity of a chain complex (the boundary of a boundary is zero).

The logical operators: \(k = 2\dim H_1(T^2; \mathbb{F}_2) = 4\)

Wait — for the toric code: \(k = 2\). For the surface code: \(k = 1\).

The logical operators correspond to non-contractible cycles on the torus — cycles that cannot be deformed to a point.

The distance: the length of the shortest non-contractible cycle \(= L\) (the lattice side length).

This geometric picture makes the distance transparent: the code distance is a topological invariant of the underlying surface.

Hypergraph product codes:

Tillich & Zémor (2014) generalized this using the hypergraph product of two classical codes:

Given classical codes with check matrices \(H_1\) and \(H_2\):

\[H_X = [H_1 \otimes I_{n_2} \mid I_{r_1} \otimes H_2^T]\] \[H_Z = [I_{n_1} \otimes H_2 \mid H_1^T \otimes I_{r_2}]\]

The parameters: \(n = n_1 n_2 + r_1 r_2\) \(k = k_1 k_2\) \(d = \min(d_1, d_2)\)

where \(n_i, k_i, d_i, r_i\) are parameters of the classical codes.

If both classical codes are LDPC with linear distance: the hypergraph product code is qLDPC with linear distance and constant rate.

BB codes are related to hypergraph product codes — the specific circulant structure is a special case of the hypergraph product construction for cyclic codes.

XI. The Quantum Singleton Bound and Its Violations

Classical Singleton bound:

A classical \([n, k, d]\) code satisfies: \(k \leq n - d + 1\)

Codes achieving this bound (MDS codes) are optimal — maximum distance for given rate.

Reed-Solomon codes achieve the Singleton bound.

Quantum Singleton bound:

For a non-degenerate \([[n, k, d]]\) code:

\[k \leq n - 4(d-1)\]

This is more restrictive than the classical bound — quantum codes pay a higher overhead for a given distance.

Degenerate codes and the bound:

Degenerate codes can potentially violate the quantum Singleton bound — their effective distance, accounting for the degeneracy, may exceed what non-degenerate codes achieve.

Whether degenerate codes actually improve the encoding rate is a subtle and active research question.

For BB codes: the parameters \([[144, 12, 12]]\) give \(k/n = 1/12\) and \(d = 12\).

The quantum Singleton bound: \(k \leq n - 4(d-1) = 144 - 44 = 100\)

So \(k = 12\) is well below the bound — the code is far from Singleton-optimal.

But the relevant comparison is not the Singleton bound — it is the surface code, which achieves \(k = 1\) for comparable \(n\) and \(d\).

XII. Connecting the Geometry to the Physics

The mathematical structures described here — symplectic geometry, chain complexes, quotient spaces, automorphism groups — are not abstract decorations.

They connect directly to physical questions.

The symplectic structure determines which operators commute — the fundamental constraint on what can be measured simultaneously.

The rank of H determines how many logical qubits are encoded — a direct consequence of the linear algebra of the stabilizer group.

The quotient space \(S^\perp/S\) is the logical Hilbert space — the space in which quantum information lives. Its symplectic structure determines the commutation relations of logical operators.

Code degeneracy determines the decoder’s task: degenerate codes have a larger equivalence class of correctable errors — potentially improving the effective threshold.

The distance is a minimum-weight problem in a coset of a linear code — connected to the geometry of the Tanner graph and the combinatorics of the stabilizer group.

The automorphism group determines the transversal gate set — which logical gates can be implemented without spreading errors.

Finding a code with good parameters and a useful automorphism group — for example, one that includes the logical T gate transversally — is the goal of the polynomial search.

This is the research program: systematic search in the space of polynomial pairs over specific groups \(\mathbb{Z}_L imes \mathbb{Z}_M\), guided by the algebraic structure, looking for codes with:

\[k \geq k_{target}, \quad d \geq d_{target}, \quad ext{Aut}(\mathcal{C}) \supseteq G_{target}\]

The space is finite but large. The constraints are computationally intensive. The rewards — a code that allows fault-tolerant universal computation with lower overhead than current approaches — are significant.

Essential reading:

Gottesman — “Stabilizer Codes and Quantum Error Correction” (1997) PhD thesis, Caltech — Chapter 2 for the symplectic formalism

Calderbank, Rains, Shor & Sloane — “Quantum Error Correction via Codes over GF(4)” (1998) IEEE Trans. Inf. Theory 44, 1369 — the symplectic/GF(4) connection

Tillich & Zémor — “Quantum LDPC Codes With Positive Rate and Minimum Distance Proportional to the Square Root of the Blocklength” (2014) IEEE Trans. Inf. Theory 60, 1193 — hypergraph product codes

Panteleev & Kalachev — “Quantum LDPC Codes With Almost Linear Minimum Distance” (2022) IEEE Trans. Inf. Theory 68, 213

Bravyi et al. — “High-Threshold and Low-Overhead Fault-Tolerant Quantum Memory” (2024) Nature 627, 778 — BB code construction and parameters

For the homological perspective:

Freedman & Hastings — “Quantum Systems on Non-k-Hyperfinite Complexes” (2013) arXiv:1301.1995

Breuckmann & Eberhardt — “Quantum Low-Density Parity-Check Codes” (2021) PRX Quantum 2, 040101 — the best review of qLDPC codes

For distance computation:

Leon — “A Probabilistic Algorithm for Computing Minimum Weights of Large Error-Correcting Codes” (1988) IEEE Trans. Inf. Theory 34, 1354

Barg & Zemor — distance bounds for random codes

For automorphisms and transversal gates:

Rains — “Quantum Codes of Minimum Distance Two” (1999) IEEE Trans. Inf. Theory 45, 266

Gottesman — “Theory of Fault-Tolerant Quantum Computation” (1998) Phys. Rev. A 57, 127
entry.summary: The deep structure of quantum error correcting codes — the symplectic geometry of Pauli space, the algebraic definition of logical operators, code degeneracy, and why the distance of a code is a geometric quantity.

entry.title: Quantum Error Correction — Protecting Quantum Information From Decoherence

link.title: Quantum Error Correction — Protecting Quantum Information From Decoherence

entry.content:
A quantum computer is a machine operating against a fundamental physical constraint.

Every qubit — every quantum bit — is in a superposition of 0 and 1. This superposition is extraordinarily fragile. A single photon from the environment, a slight vibration, a fluctuation in the electromagnetic field — any of these will collapse the superposition, destroying the quantum information.

This is decoherence.

The timescales are unforgiving. Superconducting qubits: coherence times of \(\sim 100-500\) microseconds. A quantum algorithm requires millions of operations. Each operation takes \(\sim 10-100\) nanoseconds.

The arithmetic does not work.

Without error correction, quantum computation is impossible beyond the most trivial circuits.

Quantum error correction (QEC) is the theory and practice of protecting quantum information from decoherence and operational errors.

It sits at the intersection of quantum physics, coding theory, and fault-tolerant computation — and it is the central engineering challenge of the current era of quantum computing.

I. Why Classical Error Correction Fails

Classical computers correct errors straightforwardly. Store each bit three times: 000 or 111. If you read 001, majority voting corrects to 000.

This approach fails completely for qubits. Three independent reasons.

The no-cloning theorem (Wootters & Zurek, 1982):

An unknown quantum state cannot be copied.

\[|\psi\rangle \not\to |\psi\rangle|\psi\rangle\]
for an unknown $$ \psi\rangle$$.

The proof is immediate from linearity. Suppose a unitary \(U\) clones: \(U|\psi\rangle|0\rangle = |\psi\rangle|\psi\rangle\) \(U|\phi\rangle|0\rangle = |\phi\rangle|\phi\rangle\)

Then: \(U(|\psi\rangle + |\phi\rangle)|0\rangle = |\psi\rangle|\psi\rangle + |\phi\rangle|\phi\rangle \neq (|\psi\rangle + |\phi\rangle)(|\psi\rangle + |\phi\rangle)\)

Linearity and cloning are incompatible.

The measurement problem:

To check if an error occurred, classical correction reads the bits. Reading a qubit collapses its superposition — destroying the quantum information you were trying to protect.

The continuum of errors:

A classical bit flips: 0 → 1. One type of error.

A qubit can undergo a continuum of errors: any rotation on the Bloch sphere.

\[|\psi\rangle \to (a\hat{I} + b\hat{X} + c\hat{Y} + d\hat{Z})|\psi\rangle\]

Infinitely many possible errors.

II. The Stabilizer Formalism

The solution to all three problems comes from the stabilizer formalism (Gottesman, 1997).

The Pauli group:

The single-qubit Pauli operators:

\[\hat{I} = \begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&1\end{pmatrix}, \quad \hat{X} = \begin{pmatrix}0&1\\1&0\end{pmatrix}\] \[\hat{Y} = \begin{pmatrix}0&-i\\i&0\end{pmatrix}, \quad \hat{Z} = \begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&-1\end{pmatrix}\]

The \(n\)-qubit Pauli group \(\mathcal{P}_n\): all tensor products of Pauli operators with phases \(\{\pm 1, \pm i\}\).

The stabilizer group:

A stabilizer code is defined by an abelian subgroup \(\mathcal{S} \subset \mathcal{P}_n\) that does not contain \(-\hat{I}\).

The code space \(\mathcal{C}\): the simultaneous \(+1\) eigenspace of all operators in \(\mathcal{S}\):

\[\mathcal{C} = \{|\psi\rangle : S|\psi\rangle = |\psi\rangle \; \forall S \in \mathcal{S}\}\]

Error detection:

When an error \(E \in \mathcal{P}_n\) acts on a codeword:

If \(E\) commutes with all stabilizers: the corrupted state is still in \(\mathcal{C}\) — the error is undetectable.

If \(E\) anticommutes with some stabilizer \(S_i\): measuring \(S_i\) gives eigenvalue \(-1\) — the error is detected without collapsing the logical state.

The syndrome measurement: measure all generators of \(\mathcal{S}\). The pattern of \(\pm 1\) eigenvalues — the syndrome — identifies which error occurred without revealing the logical state.

The digitization of errors:

Any error on a qubit decomposes as a linear combination of Pauli operators:

\[E = a\hat{I} + b\hat{X} + c\hat{Y} + d\hat{Z}\]

After syndrome measurement, the error collapses to one of the Pauli operators. The continuum of errors is digitized into a discrete set.

This is the fundamental mechanism that makes quantum error correction possible.

Parameters of a stabilizer code:

An \([[n, k, d]]\) stabilizer code:

  • \(n\) physical qubits
  • \(k\) logical qubits encoded
  • \(d\) code distance — minimum weight of an undetectable logical error

The code corrects any error on fewer than \(d/2\) qubits.

Rate: \(k/n\). Overhead: \(n/k\).

III. CSS Codes

A particularly important subclass: Calderbank-Shor-Steane (CSS) codes (1996).

CSS codes are defined by two classical binary linear codes \(C_1\) and \(C_2\) with \(C_2^\perp \subseteq C_1\).

The parity check matrices:

\[H_X = H(C_2^\perp), \quad H_Z = H(C_1^\perp)\]

The CSS orthogonality condition:

\[H_X H_Z^T = 0 \pmod{2}\]

X-type and Z-type stabilizers commute — a necessary condition for a valid quantum code.

The code parameters:

\[n = n_1, \quad k = k_1 + k_2 - n, \quad d = \min(d_1, d_2)\]

where \(k_i, d_i\) are the dimension and distance of the classical codes.

CSS codes have a clean separation between X-type errors (corrected by \(H_Z\)) and Z-type errors (corrected by \(H_X\)).

The Bivariate Bicycle codes described later are CSS codes — this structure is essential to their construction.

IV. The Surface Code

The dominant QEC code in current experimental implementations: the surface code (Kitaev, 1997; Dennis et al., 2002).

The construction:

Qubits on a 2D square lattice. Two types of stabilizers:

Plaquette operators (Z-type): \(A_p = \prod_{j \in \text{plaquette}} Z_j\)

Vertex operators (X-type): \(B_v = \prod_{j \in \text{star}} X_j\)

Each stabilizer acts on 4 qubits — only nearest-neighbor interactions required.

The code encodes \(k = 1\) logical qubit in \(n \approx 2d^2\) physical qubits.

The fault-tolerance threshold:

The surface code has a threshold of \(\sim 1\%\) physical error rate — among the highest of any known code.

Below threshold: increasing \(d\) exponentially suppresses the logical error rate:

\[p_L \sim \left(\frac{p}{p_{th}}\right)^{d/2}\]

For \(p = 0.1\%\) and \(p_{th} = 1\%\): at \(d = 10\): \(p_L \sim 10^{-10}\).

The overhead problem:

The surface code’s high threshold comes at a fundamental cost: low rate.

\[k/n = 1/2d^2\]

For \(d = 20\): 800 physical qubits per logical qubit. For a 1000-logical-qubit computation: \(\sim 800,000\) physical qubits required.

Current state-of-the-art hardware: \(\sim 10^3\) physical qubits.

The gap drives the search for better codes.

V. Quantum LDPC Codes

Low-Density Parity Check (LDPC) codes offer a fundamental improvement over the surface code’s overhead.

Classical LDPC codes (Gallager, 1962):

A classical LDPC code has a sparse parity-check matrix \(H\) — each row has weight \(w_r\), each column has weight \(w_c\), both small constants independent of \(n\).

Classical LDPC codes achieve the Shannon capacity with efficient decoding.

Quantum LDPC codes:

A quantum LDPC (qLDPC) code requires stabilizer generators of weight \(\leq w\) — constant, independent of \(n\).

The surface code has weight-4 stabilizers but rate \(k/n \to 0\).

The fundamental question: can we simultaneously achieve — Constant stabilizer weight — Constant rate \(k/n = \Omega(1)\) — Linear distance \(d = \Omega(n)\)?

The breakthrough (2022):

Panteleev & Kalachev (2022) and Leverrier & Zémor (2022) independently constructed good quantum LDPC codes satisfying all three:

\[k = \Omega(n), \quad d = \Omega(n), \quad \text{stabilizer weight} = O(1)\]

This establishes that the overhead of fault-tolerant quantum computation can in principle be made constant — independent of the computation size.

VI. Bivariate Bicycle Codes

Among the most promising near-term qLDPC codes: the Bivariate Bicycle (BB) codes (Bravyi et al., IBM, 2024).

The construction:

BB codes are defined over the group \(\mathbb{Z}_L \times \mathbb{Z}_M\) for integers $$L, M$.

Two polynomials over the group algebra \(\mathbb{F}_2[\mathbb{Z}_L \times \mathbb{Z}_M]\):

\[a(x, y) = \sum_{(i,j) \in A} x^i y^j, \quad b(x, y) = \sum_{(i,j) \in B} x^i y^j\]

The CSS parity check matrices:

\[H_X = [A \mid B], \quad H_Z = [B^T \mid A^T]\]

where \(A, B\) are the circulant matrices corresponding to \(a(x,y)\) and \(b(x,y)\).

The CSS orthogonality condition:

\[H_X H_Z^T = AB^T + BA^T = 0 \pmod{2}\]

Parameters:

For weight-3 polynomials (each polynomial has 3 nonzero terms):

\[n = 2LM, \quad \text{stabilizer weight} = 6\]

The rate and distance depend on the specific polynomial choice.

The [[144, 12, 12]] code:

Bravyi et al. demonstrated a \([[144, 12, 12]]\) BB code — encoding 12 logical qubits in 144 physical qubits with distance 12.

Surface code comparison: encoding 12 logical qubits with distance 12 requires \(12 \times 2 \times 144 = 3456\) physical qubits.

The BB code achieves equivalent protection with \(\approx 4\%\) of the surface code resources.

The connectivity challenge:
